questions.question: Define a distributed system and explain any three of its fundamental characteristics.
keyPoints: Distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appear as single coherent system

Three characteristics: Transparency (hides distribution), Scalability (handles growth), Fault tolerance (continues despite failures), Concurrency (multiple processes run simultaneously), Openness (supports resource sharing)

questions.question: Explain the difference between client-server and peer-to-peer architectures in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Client-server: Central server provides services, clients request services, hierarchical relationship
Peer-to-Peer: All nodes are equal, can act as both client and server, decentralized
Examples: Web browsing (client-server) vs BitTorrent (P2P)

questions.question: What is middleware in distributed systems? Explain its role and list any two examples.
keyPoints: Middleware is software layer between applications and operating system/network
Role: Provides communication services, handles heterogeneity, manages coordination
Examples: CORBA, Java RMI, .NET Remoting, Message queues

questions.question: Discuss three advantages and three disadvantages of distributed systems.
keyPoints: Advantages: Resource sharing, reliability through redundancy, scalability, openness, cost-effectiveness
Disadvantages: Complex software development, network security issues, difficult troubleshooting, higher infrastructure costs, data integrity challenges

questions.question: What is clock synchronization in distributed systems? Explain any one algorithm used for clock synchronization.
keyPoints: Clock synchronization ensures processes agree on common time
Cristian's algorithm: Client requests time from time server, calculates round-trip delay, adjusts local clock
NTP (Network Time Protocol): Hierarchical system of time servers for better accuracy

questions.question: Explain the concept of transparency in distributed systems. List at least four types of transparency.
keyPoints: Transparency hides distribution complexity from users and applications
Types: Access transparency, Location transparency, Replication transparency, Migration transparency, Concurrency transparency, Failure transparency

questions.question: What is distributed mutual exclusion? State why it is needed and mention one algorithm to achieve it.
keyPoints: Distributed mutual exclusion ensures only one process accesses shared resource at a time
Needed to prevent race conditions and maintain consistency in distributed systems
Algorithms: Lamport's algorithm (timestamp-based), Ricart-Agrawala (token-based), Maekawa's algorithm

questions.question: Explain the architecture of distributed systems with the help of a diagram. Discuss the role of the client-server model and three-tier architecture.
keyPoints: Diagram should show clients, servers, network, middleware layers
Client-server model: Clients request services, servers provide resources, request-response pattern
Three-tier architecture: Presentation tier (client UI), Application tier (business logic), Data tier (database)
Advantages of three-tier: Scalability, maintainability, better security

questions.question: Discuss fault tolerance in distributed systems. Explain the techniques used to achieve fault tolerance including replication and checkpointing.
keyPoints: Fault tolerance: System's ability to continue operation despite failures
Replication: Maintaining multiple copies of data/processes on different nodes
Checkpointing: Saving system state periodically for recovery after failure
Other techniques: Redundancy, failover mechanisms, rollback recovery
Byzantine failures, fail-stop failures

questions.question: Explain Remote Procedure Call (RPC) in distributed systems. How does it work? Discuss its advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: RPC allows programs to call procedures on remote machines as if they were local
Working: Client stub marshals parameters, sends request to server, server executes, returns result
Advantages: Location transparency, simplified distributed programming
Limitations: Network latency, partial failure handling, heterogeneity issues

questions.question: What is a Distributed File System (DFS)? Explain its architecture and discuss any two examples of DFS.
keyPoints: DFS allows files to be stored across multiple networked computers
Architecture: Client module, Directory service, Storage servers, Coherency module
Examples: NFS (Network File System) - Unix-based, AFS (Andrew File System) - wide-area, GFS (Google File System) - distributed storage for large-scale data processing
Benefits: Fault tolerance, scalability, load balancing
